macro_segments.title: The Mishna on Three Wives with Different Ketubah Amounts and the Estate Division Problem

micro_segments.title: Introduction and Setup of the Mishna: Three Women Married on the Same Day with Different Ketubah Claims
First Case: Estate Worth 100, Three Claims of 100, 200, and 300 - Equal Division Among All
Second Case: Estate Worth 200 - Distribution of 50, 75, 75 and the Logical Problem It Presents
Third Case: Estate Worth 300 - Distribution of 50, 100, 150 Following Proportional Claims
The Distinction Between Two Models: Contested Amount Principle vs. Proportional Investment Model

macro_segments.title: Robert Aumann's Game-Theoretic Solution to the Mishna's Division Problem

micro_segments.title: Introduction to Robert Aumann and His Paper on the Three Women Case
The Principle of Shnayim Ochzim (Two Claimants): Focusing on What Is Conceded vs. What Is Disputed
Aumann's Insight: The Solution Works When Examined in Pairs of Claimants
Working Backwards: How 50, 75, 75 and 50, 100, 150 Follow the Pairwise Principle
Verification: Demonstrating That All Pairwise Scenarios (100-200, 200-300, 100-300) Satisfy the Disputed Amount Rule
The Uniqueness Theorem: Why This Is the Only Solution That Works for All Pairwise Combinations

macro_segments.title: The Gemara's Attempts to Explain the Mishna Through Ad Hoc Interpretations

micro_segments.title: The Gemara's Problem: Why Does the 100 Woman Get 50 Instead of 33⅓?
Shmuel's First Solution: Mrs. 200 Waives Her Claim Against Mrs. 100 in the First 100
Resolving the Contradiction: How Waiving a Claim Works and Its Logical Limits
Shmuel's Second Solution: Mrs. 300 Removes Herself from the First 100
Rabbi Yaakov's Explanation: Staged Seizure Model (Two Acquisitions)
Working Through the Staged Model: How 75 Seized First, Then 225 Seized Second

macro_segments.title: The Opinion of Rabbi: Proportional Distribution and Rebbi's Implicit Adoption of the Mishna

micro_segments.title: Rabbi's Statement: 'I Do Not Understand Rabbi Natan's Words' and His Alternative of Equal Division
The Question of Why the Mishna Presents Rabbi Natan's View: Authorial Authority in the Mishnaic Tradition
Tosafot's Interpretation: Cholkim B'Shaveh Means Proportional Division by Claim Size

macro_segments.title: Investment Partnership (Hitilu LaKis) and the Debate Over Divisible vs. Non-Divisible Assets

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Teaching: Two Partners Invest 100 and 200; All Profits Divided Equally
Rava's Limitation: The Indivisible Asset Model - The Case of a Plow Ox
Rav Hamnuna's Counter-Position: Equal Division Even When the Asset Becomes Divisible
The Gemara's Resolution: Distinguishing Between an Ox Bought for Plowing vs. Slaughtering
Tosafta's Clarification: When Mixed Discrete Objects Are Present, Division Is Proportional

macro_segments.title: Connecting the Investment Rule to the Three Wives Mishna and the Question of Intrinsic Value

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's Final Rule: When Profit or Loss Occurs in Investments, Division Is Proportional

The Implication: Proportional Division Applies When Dealing with Principal Value Changes
